items.myth: 100% training accuracy means the model is ready for production.
items.reality: Perfect training scores often indicate memorization or an overly flexible model. Generalization requires strong held-out test or validation performance — not training-set perfection.
items.example: A deep tree hits 100% on 200 training rows but 62% on 500 held-out rows — the model memorized, it did not generalize.

items.myth: Generalization and overfitting are unrelated concepts.
items.reality: Overfitting is a primary cause of poor generalization: low training error paired with high test error. Good generalization means the model captures signal that transfers to unseen data.
items.example: Train error 0.02, test error 0.35 — classic overfitting pattern and a failed generalization story despite excellent training metrics.

items.myth: If cross-validation score is high, the model will generalize forever without retraining.
items.reality: CV estimates generalization under the i.i.d. assumption for the data at hand. Distribution shift, new products, or changing user behavior can degrade real-world performance over time.
items.example: A 2020 mobility model scored well in CV but failed after pandemic travel patterns shifted — offline generalization did not transfer to the new regime.

items.myth: Unseen data means any random split of the same file is equally valid.
items.reality: Generalization estimates are valid only when held-out rows are truly excluded from training and drawn from the same problem distribution — temporal leakage or duplicate users across splits inflate scores.
items.example: Splitting click logs randomly while the same user appears in train and test inflates metrics; user-level hold-out gives a honest generalization estimate.
